name: listComments_multiword_path_param
_note: Multi-word path param `{postId}`. Regression guard: the Python identifier is `post_id`, which binds ONLY via the generated `Path(alias="postId")` — without it FastAPI 422s (a latent bug compile-lint can't catch, since the code is valid Python). Also proves Go/TS bind a multi-word path param. The handler echoes back one comment; page/limit are explicit (non-default) so the query round-trip is observable too.

name: getPostMetered_request_and_response_headers
_note: Request headers reach the handler as args (asserted via expect_received, exactly like query/path params): authorization (auto wire name Authorization), requestId (override X-Request-Id), ifNoneMatch (optional, OMITTED here -> handler sees null), maxStale (a defaulted header; the generated client always sends it as a value, so the driver supplies the default 60 explicitly — see the 'defaulted request headers' note in README). Response is a typed envelope: body + response headers (ratelimitRemaining via X-RateLimit-Remaining, etag optional). returns_headers are the values the stub sets; ok_headers are what the client must read back off the response.

name: getPostMetered_optional_request_header_present
_note: Optional request header ifNoneMatch IS supplied this time, and maxStale is explicitly sent (non-default), proving optional + defaulted request headers round-trip. Response etag is OMITTED by the stub -> client reads it back as null/absent.

name: uploadAvatar_multipart_file_and_fields
_note: Multipart upload. call.multipart carries file parts (content as a UTF-8 string the driver encodes to bytes) and scalar form fields. Scalar `fields` are JSON-typed (string caption, Int rotation, Bool crop) so the round-trip exercises each target's scalar coercion end-to-end (client stringification — notably bool → "true"/"false" — and server parse back to the typed value). The handler asserts it received the file bytes + filename and each scalar via expect_received: files are checked by their decoded UTF-8 content under <field>_content; the optional thumbnail is omitted here (handler sees it absent/null). Response is a normal JSON struct (Author).

name: uploadAvatar_multipart_optional_thumbnail_present
_note: Multipart upload that SUPPLIES the optional thumbnail file (companion to uploadAvatar_multipart_file_and_fields, which omits it). Exercises the client's optional-file-present branch (it appends the second file part) and the server's optional-file assign-on-success branch. Uses crop=false (the companion uses crop=true) so both Bool wire encodings are exercised. The handler asserts both files' decoded content and filename plus the scalars; thumbnail_filename is unobservable in the TS target (Blob carries no filename) and is skipped there.

name: uploadAvatar_multipart_constraint_empty_caption
_note: Multipart upload with an empty caption, which violates AvatarUpload's `caption: String where self.length > 0`. Proves a multipart scalar-field constraint is enforced server-side on EVERY target — the divergence that used to be Go-only (Python now binds `Form(..., min_length=1)`; TypeScript now runs `validateUploadAvatarBody` over the assembled body, which checks scalar constraints and ignores Blob file fields). The handler must NOT be called; the client sees a 400 (Go/TS) / 422 (Python). rotation/crop are valid so caption is the sole offender.

name: downloadAvatar_binary_response
_note: Binary download. The response body is raw bytes (not JSON). handler.returns_file is the UTF-8 content the stub streams back; expect_download is what the client must read off the response body. No JSON response struct is involved.

name: listTaggedPosts_version_prefix
_note: Versioned endpoint declared under `api version "v2" { }`. The generated path is /v2/api/posts/tagged/{tag}. This case proves the prefix round-trips: the generated client builds the /v2/... URL and the generated server registers the /v2/... route — if they disagreed on the prefix the request would 404 and this case would fail. No explicit path assertion is needed; mutual success IS the proof. Exercises a path param (tag) + a defaulted query param under the prefix.

name: listPostsOffset_pagination_envelope
_note: Offset pagination. The response is the <Endpoint>Page envelope { items, totalCount }, not a bare list. handler.returns IS the page object the stub returns; expect_client.ok is the page the client must read back off the response body — proving the handler-supplied metadata (totalCount) round-trips. page/limit are ordinary query params (Approach 2). Wire field names are items/totalCount (camelCase) on Go/TS/OpenAPI; the Python target uses snake_case on the wire for all model fields (pre-existing convention) — each driver compares using its own target's field shape.

name: listPostsCursor_pagination_envelope
_note: Cursor pagination. Response is the <Endpoint>Page envelope { items, nextCursor? }. This case supplies a non-null nextCursor (more pages follow) and an explicit cursor input, proving the cursor metadata round-trips through the body.

name: listPostsCursor_last_page_null_cursor
_note: Cursor pagination, LAST page: nextCursor is null (no more pages). Exercises the null-cursor wire path the non-null case above doesn't — Go (`*string`) and Python (`str | None`) serialize it as JSON null, so this proves a null nextCursor round-trips through the body and back into the typed envelope. Matches the OpenAPI page schema, where nextCursor is nullable (anyOf [string, null]).

name: upsertPost2_multi_status_created
_note: Multi-status: the handler returns the <Endpoint>Response envelope { status, body }. Here the stub returns 201 with a Post body; the client must read BOTH the status (201) and the body back. handler.returns_status is the status the stub sets; handler.returns is the body; expect_client.status + expect_client.ok are what the client must observe.

name: upsertPost2_multi_status_no_content
_note: Multi-status typeless branch: the stub returns 204 with NO body. The client must observe status 204 and an absent/null body. returns_status = 204, no returns; expect_client.status = 204, no ok (body absent).

name: upsertPost2_multi_status_updated
_note: Multi-status 200 branch: the stub returns 200 (updated existing) with a Post body, completing coverage of the typed statuses (201 created is covered above). The client must read status 200 plus the body.

name: upsertPost2_multi_status_accepted_no_body
_note: Multi-status NON-204 typeless branch: the stub returns 202 with NO body. Pins that clients detect the empty body by CONTENT, not by special-casing 204 — a status-code guard would try to parse the empty 202 body and throw.

name: upsertPost2_undeclared_status
_note: Server-side handler-bug guard: the stub returns a status (418) outside the declared {200, 201, 202, 204} set. The generated server must refuse to write it and answer 500 ('handler returned undeclared status') — a smuggled non-2xx would bypass error { }. The TS client surfaces an unmapped status as ApiError('Unknown', ...).

name: upsertPost2_body_for_bodyless_status
_note: Server-side handler-bug guard, body-shape direction: the stub pairs a body with the TYPELESS 204. Only 204/304 (plus 1xx in Go) get framework body suppression — on a typeless 202 or 205 the body would reach the wire and silently violate the contract — so the server rejects the shape mismatch itself with a 500 ('handler returned a body for a bodyless status') for ANY typeless status.

name: upsertPost2_no_body_for_typed_status
_note: Server-side handler-bug guard, body-shape direction: the stub returns the TYPED 200 with NO body. The contract (and the OpenAPI spec) promise a Post body on 200, so the server answers 500 ('handler returned no body for a typed status') instead of writing a bodyless 200 the client would surface as body=None.

name: upsertPost2_error_validation
_note: Multi-status + error { }: the stub raises ValidationError instead of returning an envelope. Pins that the declared-error mapping still works on a multi-status endpoint — the Go server re-emits its own variant→status mapping inside the multi-status branch, so this is the only runtime coverage of that code path. Independent of the envelope guards above (no returns_status at all here).

name: requeuePost_accepted
_note: ALL-TYPELESS multi-status block (response { 202 204 }): the RequeuePostResponse envelope is just { status } with NO body field — the client must record the status without ever touching the response body. This is the no-shared-T codegen branch the mixed upsertPost2 endpoint cannot reach.

name: requeuePost_noop
_note: All-typeless block, second declared status: the stub picks 204 (no-op) and the client reads it back off the status-only envelope.

name: upsertPost2_undeclared_2xx_client_lenient
_note: CLIENT LENIENCY (design-decisions.md 'Clients are deliberately lenient'): a proxy or middlebox can rewrite a success status to a 2xx the contract never declared, and the client must envelope whatever 2xx arrives rather than fail. The generated server can never send an undeclared status (its guard answers 500), so raw_response short-circuits it: the driver answers every request itself with this canned status+body and the generated handler is NEVER invoked (no handler fields; the ok-case handler-was-called assertion is skipped). 203 is undeclared on upsertPost2 (declared: 200/201/202/204).

name: requeuePost_undeclared_2xx_client_lenient
_note: Client leniency on the ALL-TYPELESS envelope: a non-standard 2xx (299) with no body, undeclared on requeuePost (declared: 202/204), served via raw_response (generated handler never invoked). The status-only envelope must carry 299 through without the client touching the (empty) body.

name: syncCatalog_roundtrips_composite_types
_note: Composite type-shape round-trip the rest of gen_api.phx doesn't cover: a Map<String,String> (labels), a List<enum> (allowedStatuses), and a List<struct> as a FIELD (entries, each carrying an enum). The stub ECHOES the decoded body into the Catalog response, so expect_client.ok == call.body and the existing deep-JSON assertion proves the shapes survive BOTH the client->server and server->client legs. The `ownerTeam` label key is deliberately camelCase: it is a Map VALUE key (data, not a struct field), so generators must pass it through verbatim. The Go/TS drivers deep-equal exactly and prove that; the Python driver normalizes both sides symmetrically (it can't distinguish a map key from a field), so it can't catch map-key mangling on its own — Go/TS cover it. The `class` (String) and `async` (Bool) fields are RESERVED-WORD field names: the Python generator escapes them to `class_`/`async_` and serializes by that name (no alias), so the Python WIRE key diverges from Go/TS — the round-trip proves the escaped Python field serializes AND decodes (the wire-key contract here is camelCase `class`/`async`, which Go's JSON tags and TS's verbatim properties match directly, and the Python driver maps to via its keyword-aware to_snake). This is the runtime guard for keyword field names that compile-lint alone can't provide.
